summary: Fernando Pérez presents a comprehensive vision for reproducible scientific research, drawing from his experience teaching STAT 159 at UC Berkeley and the development of Project Jupyter's Binder infrastructure. He traces the intellectual lineage of reproducibility from Claerbout and Donoho in the 1990s through to modern tools, arguing that reproducibility must be a daily practice rather than a publication-time afterthought. Using a real-world case study of a Norwegian wind patterns paper, he demonstrates how even well-intentioned open science efforts suffer from bit rot. He showcases Binder as a tool that packages computational environments into one-click executable links, exemplified by the LIGO gravitational waves discovery notebooks. The talk emphasizes lowering barriers to access for scientists worldwide, particularly those without institutional resources.
key_points: Reproducibility must be built into daily research practice from day one, not treated as a cleanup task at publication time
A reproducible research pattern includes version control, open languages, automation (Make), dependency management, testing, continuous integration, and Binder for sharing
Even a well-executed open science paper from 2016 broke within a year due to subtle version dependencies, demonstrating the fragility of computational reproducibility
Binder reduces the barrier to accessing scientific computation to just an internet connection and a browser, with global reach shown by worldwide usage data
The tools built for open science also serve as leverage against predatory scientific publishers by giving the community alternatives on legal grounds
money_quotes: An article about computational science in a scientific publication is not the scholarship itself, it is merely advertising of the scholarship.
Reproducibility is not a publication time concept. Either you do it from day one as a daily practice, or you just don't do it.
Untested code is broken code. If you don't have tests, you don't know that it works.
If the purpose of science is to sort of open the black box of nature, doing that with a process and tools that are not themselves open and available to others is kind of an oxymoron.
Git is kind of the moral equivalent of making the difference between brushing your teeth and a root canal.

A hundred years of theoretical physics, 30 years of building instruments, a collaboration building this analysis with open-source tools. And here, with one click of the mouse, any of you can be actually running the analysis of the LIGO collaboration.
What if you gave me as part of the review process the PDF and the binder link and I'm reviewing the live real analysis and I can change it?
I want to be able to share back with someone like my former advisor from Colombia, who doesn't necessarily have the budgetary freedom that I have at a big fancy US university.
key_insight: Reproducible research requires embedding version control, testing, dependency management, and automation into daily scientific practice from day one, and tools like Binder can make the results accessible to anyone with a browser.
